questions.question: According to the lesson, what is self-efficacy and why is it the strongest predictor of exercise adherence?

options.text: Self-efficacy is your physical fitness level, which determines if you can exercise
Self-efficacy is your belief in your ability to successfully exercise, and it predicts adherence independent of baseline fitness, depression severity, or environmental barriers
Self-efficacy is the amount of motivation you feel before exercising
Self-efficacy only matters for competitive athletes, not regular people

questions.explanation: Self-efficacy is your belief in your ability to successfully perform a behavior. In exercise research, it's the strongest predictor of adherence — even more important than baseline fitness level or depression severity. If you don't believe you can exercise, you won't sustain it, even with perfect knowledge. Building self-efficacy through small wins is critical.

questions.question: What is the most important principle when determining the 'best' exercise program?

options.text: The program with the highest intensity and longest duration
The program that a fitness influencer recommends
The best exercise program is the one you'll actually do — matching your current capacity, intrinsic motivations, barriers, and facilitators
Any program works equally well for everyone

questions.explanation: Adherence is the most critical variable. The 'best' program isn't the most optimal on paper — it's the one you'll actually sustain. This means it must match your current capacity (not where you 'should' be), align with your intrinsic motivations, account for your specific barriers, and build on your facilitators.

questions.question: If you're currently sedentary (0-2 days/week of movement), what should your primary focus be?

options.text: Immediately start with 60-minute daily workouts to catch up
Focus on consistency before duration or intensity — start with the absolute minimum viable dose like 10 minutes, 3x/week
Only do high-intensity exercise to get maximum results quickly
Wait until you feel motivated before starting

questions.explanation: If you're sedentary or minimally active, your biggest win is establishing the habit through consistency. Start with 10 minutes, 3x/week. Focus on showing up, not optimizing duration or intensity. Once consistency is established, you can gradually increase. Trying to do too much too soon leads to burnout and dropout.

questions.question: Based on this assessment lesson, identify ONE barrier that most often prevents you from moving, and ONE facilitator you could amplify immediately. How will these insights inform your movement plan?
questions.explanation: This reflection helps you apply the lesson's assessment framework to your own situation. Common barriers include low energy, time constraints, cost, or fear of judgment. Common facilitators include scheduled classes, accountability partners, tracking progress, outdoor settings, or music. The principle is: don't just address barriers — amplify what already works for you.

questions.question: What's the difference between extrinsic and intrinsic motivation, and which sustains behavior long-term?

options.text: Extrinsic (external rewards, 'should') is stronger and more sustainable than intrinsic motivation
Intrinsic motivation (internal values, enjoyment, meaning) sustains behavior long-term, while extrinsic motivation (guilt, external authority) fades quickly
Both types of motivation are equally effective for long-term adherence
Motivation doesn't matter — only discipline matters

questions.explanation: Extrinsic motivation (external rewards, guilt, 'should') may work short-term but fades quickly. Intrinsic motivation (connecting movement to personal values — better sleep, less anxiety, feeling strong) sustains behavior long-term. The lesson emphasizes strengthening your intrinsic 'why' by completing: 'I want to move because it would help me _______' with personally meaningful answers.

title: Building Your Personal Movement Assessment - Quiz
